Title: Biosynthesis of redox-active metabolites in response to iron deficiency in plants

Common_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: ABC transporter / pleiotropic drug resistance transporter
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Root-exuded redox-active coumarin that mobilizes iron under iron deficiency
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Principal exporter of sideretin from roots; mutation strongly reduces sideretin exudation and causes root accumulation of sideretin glucosides with impaired iron nutrition under alkaline conditions
Common_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Iron deficiency; alkaline/high-pH growth conditions accentuate phenotype
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Mutant metabolite profiling of spent medium and roots; soil and agar phenotyping under altered Fe availability
Common_Genes.Omics_Data: Comparative metabolomics by LC-MS; no accession given
Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: pdr9-2 mutants show chlorosis, stunting, lower chlorophyll and shoot Fe under low-availability iron conditions
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Spent medium sideretin is ~10-fold lower in pdr9-2, while root sideretin glucosides are elevated
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. PDR9 is the principal root exporter for sideretin. 2. pdr9-2 mutants show strongly reduced sideretin in exudates and increased sideretin glucosides in roots. 3. Efficient export of catecholic coumarins is necessary for optimal iron acquisition under challenging conditions.

Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Hydroxylates feruloyl-CoA at the 6' position to form the precursor leading to scopoletin biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Redox-active catecholic coumarins that mobilize iron for plant uptake under iron deficiency
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Required for production of scopoletin-derived catecholic coumarins; loss abolishes sideretin/fraxetin pathway metabolites and impairs iron nutrition under low Fe/high pH
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Not established; pathway-entry enzyme required for scopoletin-derived coumarin biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Upregulated in iron-deficient roots; previously reported in root epidermis and cortex
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: T-DNA knockout metabolite profiling; heterologous transient expression in Nicotiana benthamiana; rescue with exogenous coumarins; phenotyping on alkaline soil/agar
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: Comparative metabolomics of root exudates and tissues by HPLC-ESI-MS/LC-MS; transcriptome data mining from AtGenExpress and Dinneny et al.; no accession given
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: f6'h1 mutants show chlorosis, reduced chlorophyll and shoot Fe under iron-limiting alkaline conditions
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Expression of F6'H1 alone in N. benthamiana generated scopoletin and also esculetin; f6'h1 mutants lacked all major oxidized coumarins in exudates
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. F6'H1 is required upstream of the sideretin pathway because f6'h1 mutants lacked compounds derived from scopoletin in root exudates. 2. Transient expression of F6'H1 in N. benthamiana produced scopoletin, establishing pathway entry. 3. Loss of F6'H1 impaired iron nutrition under alkaline/iron-limiting conditions, linking coumarin biosynthesis to Fe acquisition.

Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Hydroxylates scopoletin at the 8 position to form fraxetin
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Redox-active catecholic coumarins that solubilize and reduce Fe(III) to support iron uptake
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Required to convert scopoletin to catecholic coumarins; mutants exude scopoletin but not fraxetin/sideretin and are impaired in iron nutrition under alkaline conditions
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Key committed step toward catecholic coumarins from non-catecholic scopoletin
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Strongly induced by iron deficiency in roots; promoter activity along primary and lateral roots except apical zones; strongest in root epidermis
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Strongly induced by iron deficiency in roots
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: T-DNA knockout metabolite profiling; qPCR; promoter-GUS/GFP expression analysis; transient pathway reconstitution in Nicotiana benthamiana; rescue with exogenous fraxetin/sideretin
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: Comparative metabolomics by LC-MS; transcriptome coexpression mining from AtGenExpress and Dinneny et al.; no accession given
Pathway_Genes.Applied_Promoters: Native S8H promoter (2023 bp) used for GUS and 3xGFP reporter constructs
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: s8h mutants show chlorosis, reduced chlorophyll and Fe on alkaline substrate/Fe-limiting conditions
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Active recombinant protein could not be readily obtained in vitro due to instability; functional identity was established by genetics and heterologous pathway reconstruction
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. At3g12900 was identified as an iron deficiency-induced 2-ODD coexpressed with CYP82C4 and named S8H. 2. s8h mutants accumulated/exuded scopoletin but not further oxidized coumarins, indicating loss of scopoletin 8-hydroxylation. 3. Coexpression of F6'H1 and S8H in N. benthamiana produced fraxetin, confirming S8H function and its necessity for catecholic coumarin-mediated iron uptake.

Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Hydroxylates fraxetin to produce sideretin, which readily oxidizes to its quinone form
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Redox-active root-exuded coumarin that mobilizes iron by chelation/reduction under iron deficiency
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Catalyzes final step to sideretin; mutants accumulate fraxetin instead of sideretin and can perform as well as or better than wild type at high pH
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Terminal determinant, but not beneficial under all conditions; condition-dependent value
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Strongly induced by iron deficiency in roots; promoter active along primary and lateral roots except apical zones; strongest in root epidermis
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Strongly induced by iron deficiency in roots
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Iron deficiency; pH-dependent effect with sideretin favored at low pH and fraxetin relatively advantageous at high pH
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: T-DNA knockout metabolite profiling; qPCR; yeast microsome enzyme assay with fraxetin and NADPH; transient pathway reconstitution in Nicotiana benthamiana; promoter-GUS/GFP analysis; plant phenotyping
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: Comparative metabolomics by LC-MS; transcriptome coexpression mining from AtGenExpress and Dinneny et al.; no accession given
Pathway_Genes.Applied_Promoters: Native CYP82C4 promoter (1151 bp) used for GUS and 3xGFP reporter constructs
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Conversion of fraxetin to sideretin may be maladaptive under alkaline conditions; cyp82C4 mutants sometimes outperform wild type at high pH
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: CYP82C4 ortholog is missing in some Brassicaceae such as Eutrema salsugineum; this correlates with absence of sideretin production
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. CYP82C4 is strongly induced by iron deficiency and is required for converting fraxetin to sideretin. 2. cyp82C4 mutants lack sideretin and accumulate fraxetin, while yeast microsomes expressing CYP82C4 convert fraxetin to sideretin-like products. 3. CYP82C4 defines the terminal step of sideretin biosynthesis, but its benefit is pH dependent and may be disadvantageous under alkaline conditions.
